family: Fries-, Photo-Fries, and Anionic Ortho-Fries Rearrangement
rationale: The Fries rearrangement of phenyl acetate to 2-hydroxyacetophenone is the simplest and most frequently cited textbook example. It clearly demonstrates the core transformation: the migration of an acyl group from a phenolic oxygen to an ortho position on the aromatic ring, catalyzed by a Lewis acid (AlCl3). This example is minimal, acyclic in its key functional groups, and unambiguously represents the Fries rearrangement.
collision_avoidance_note: This example uses the simplest possible phenyl ester (phenyl acetate) to avoid complex aromatic systems that could overlap with other named reactions involving arenes. The acyl group is a simple acetyl group, avoiding reactive motifs. The transformation is a specific rearrangement of an ester to a ketone on a phenol, which is highly characteristic of the Fries family and distinct from Claisen rearrangements (which involve allyl ethers) or other electrophilic aromatic substitutions.
